1:
On Galatians 1:1 — “Paul, an apostle, (not of men, neither by man, but by Jesus Christ, and God the Father, who raised him from the dead)” — the witnesses mark the assertion of a divine apostleship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the blunt denial. Robertson: “not from men, neither through men” — the bluntness is due to the Judaizers’ charge that Paul was no genuine apostle, not one of the twelve. Ellicott: the title is taken “in its strictest sense.” The Lutheran Luther: Paul’s arguments “tend to the advancing… of his ministry, and… to the discrediting” of the false apostles. The Eastern Chrysostom: this epistle “breathes an indignant spirit.”

The verse opens the letter with Paul’s emphatic claim. He is an apostle not from men nor through any man, but directly by Jesus Christ and God the Father. His commission is wholly divine.

So the witnesses see Paul, from the first word, defend the divine origin of his apostleship against those who would discredit it. The witnesses note the unusual bluntness: where other letters open mildly, here Paul at once denies any human source for his office — “not of men” (not from human authority) “neither by man” (not through any human agent), “but by Jesus Christ, and God the Father.” They mark the occasion Chrysostom and Luther identify: the Judaizers had urged that Paul, not being one of the twelve, was an inferior apostle whose gospel might be corrected. Against this, Paul stakes his authority on a direct, heavenly call. They observe the pointed mention of God “who raised him from the dead” — the risen Christ who appeared to Paul on the Damascus road is the very one who commissioned him. The verse thus sets the tone and theme of the whole epistle: Paul’s gospel is no human invention, for his apostleship is no human appointment. The indignant defence of his divine commission, begun here, will run through the first two chapters — the ground on which he will insist that the gospel of grace he preached is the one true gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul, from the first word, defend the divine origin of his apostleship. The unusual bluntness — “not of men, neither by man” — answers the Judaizers’ charge that he, not being one of the twelve, was an inferior apostle whose gospel might be corrected. Against this he stakes his authority on a direct, heavenly call. The pointed mention of God “who raised him from the dead” recalls that the risen Christ who appeared on the Damascus road is the very one who commissioned him. The indignant defence begun here runs through the first two chapters.
2:
On Galatians 1:2 — “And all the brethren which are with me, unto the churches of Galatia” — the witnesses mark the companions and the recipients.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the address. Robertson: “all the brethren… with me” — probably the small company of travelling companions; “unto the churches of Galatia” — a circular letter to all the churches there. Clarke: his assistants in preaching. The Reformed Calvin: he writes in the name of many, that the churches might listen to a greater number. The Lutheran Luther: this stops the mouths of the false apostles.

The verse names the senders and addressees. All the brethren with Paul join in the greeting, sent to the churches of Galatia. The letter is addressed to a whole region of congregations.

So the witnesses see Paul associate his companions with him and direct the letter to the Galatian churches. The witnesses note that, unlike letters where he names individuals, here Paul speaks generally of “all the brethren which are with me” — likely his travelling associates, who agreed with what he wrote. They mark Calvin’s and Luther’s point that joining a body of brethren lends weight to his testimony against the false teachers: the truth he defends is not his alone but shared by all the faithful with him. They observe that “the churches of Galatia” marks this as a circular letter, addressed not to one congregation but to several throughout the region — a sign of how widely the Judaizing error had spread. They note the absence of the warm commendations with which Paul often opens his letters; the gravity of the Galatians’ defection leaves no room for the usual praise. The verse thus frames the epistle’s scope and tone: a solemn corporate witness, sent to a whole province of churches endangered by a false gospel — preparing the brief salutation of grace and the abrupt rebuke that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul associate his companions with him and direct the letter to a whole region. The “brethren which are with me” are likely his travelling associates, who agreed with what he wrote; joining a body of brethren lends weight to his witness. “The churches of Galatia” marks a circular letter, a sign of how widely the error had spread. They note the absence of his usual warm commendation — the gravity of the defection leaves no room for praise — framing the epistle’s solemn corporate tone.
3:
On Galatians 1:3 — “Grace be to you and peace from God the Father, and from our Lord Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the apostolic salutation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the customary greeting. Robertson: “grace to you and peace” — not a perfunctory thing with Paul (cf. Romans 1:7). Ellicott traces the widening sense of “Father.” The Reformed Calvin: Paul wishes them “friendship with God, and… all good things,” the favour of God being the source of all prosperity. The Lutheran Luther expounds the terms grace and peace at length.

The verse gives the familiar greeting. Grace and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. Even the salutation carries the gospel Paul will defend.

So the witnesses see in the customary greeting more than a formality. The witnesses note that “grace and peace” is Paul’s habitual salutation, yet, as Robertson insists, never perfunctory: grace is the free favour of God, the fountain of all blessing, and peace its fruit, the reconciled state of the soul with God. They mark Calvin’s observation that Paul wishes the Galatians the whole good that flows from God’s favour. They observe that both gifts come jointly “from God the Father, and from our Lord Jesus Christ” — the Father and the Son named together as the one source, an implicit testimony to Christ’s deity. They note Luther’s care to expound grace and peace as the sum of the Christian’s blessedness, the very things the false gospel of works would destroy. The verse thus opens with the heart of the gospel in seed: grace, the free favour that justifies, and peace, its result — the gifts Paul will spend the letter defending against the legalism that would put salvation on the footing of works rather than grace. Even the greeting prepares the great theme.

So the witnesses see in the customary greeting more than a formality. “Grace and peace” is Paul’s habitual salutation, yet never perfunctory: grace the free favour of God, the fountain of all blessing, and peace its fruit. Both come jointly “from God the Father, and from our Lord Jesus Christ” — named together as one source, an implicit testimony to Christ’s deity. Luther expounds grace and peace as the sum of the Christian’s blessedness — the very things the false gospel of works would destroy. Even the greeting prepares the great theme.
4:
On Galatians 1:4 — “Who gave himself for our sins, that he might deliver us from this present evil world, according to the will of God and our Father” — the witnesses mark the heart of the gospel set early.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the placement. Barnes: Paul introduces this cardinal doctrine at once because it was “the great truth… ever to be kept before the mind.” Robertson: “for our sins” (1 Corinthians 15:3; Galatians 2:20); “deliver” (rescue). Adam Clarke: “a sin-offering… that they might be saved.” The Lutheran Luther: Paul “hath nothing in his mouth but Christ.”

The verse states the gospel at the outset. Christ gave himself for our sins, to deliver us from this present evil age, according to the Father’s will. Redemption by his self-sacrifice is the foundation of all.

So the witnesses see Paul plant the heart of the gospel in the very greeting. The witnesses note the early placement: before any argument, Paul names the cardinal truth — Christ “gave himself for our sins,” a free, voluntary, atoning self-offering, the just for the unjust. They mark the purpose: “that he might deliver us from this present evil world” — not from the creation, but from the guilt, corruption, and bondage of the present sinful age, with its vain customs and the slavery of sin. They observe that all this is “according to the will of God and our Father” — the redemption springs from the Father’s own gracious purpose, not from any merit of ours. They note Luther’s observation that Paul cannot speak without Christ, every word breathing his fervency. The verse thus sets the gospel of grace against the legalism Paul will combat: salvation rests on Christ’s self-giving for sins, by the Father’s will, delivering us from this evil age — not on the works of the law. The whole epistle’s argument is anticipated in this opening confession of free, sovereign, redeeming grace.

So the witnesses see Paul plant the heart of the gospel in the greeting. Christ “gave himself for our sins,” a free, atoning self-offering; the purpose, “that he might deliver us from this present evil world” — from the guilt, corruption, and bondage of the sinful age, not the creation. And all “according to the will of God” — springing from the Father’s gracious purpose, not our merit. Luther notes Paul cannot speak without Christ. The gospel of grace is set against the legalism Paul will combat: salvation rests on Christ’s self-giving, not the works of the law.
5:
On Galatians 1:5 — “To whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen” — the witnesses mark the doxology.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ascription. Robertson: no verb in the Greek, like other doxologies (Romans 11:36; 1 Timothy 1:17). Barnes: Paul often introduces praise in the midst of argument, from his strong desire that all glory be given to God. Adam Clarke: glory to him for delivering us from bondage to Mosaic rites. The Reformed Calvin: by this thanksgiving he stirs them to contemplate the invaluable gift received.

The verse breaks into praise. To God be glory for ever. The thought of redeeming grace overflows into worship.

So the witnesses see Paul’s heart overflow into doxology at the thought of grace. The witnesses note his frequent habit of bursting into praise in the midst of his argument, so full is he of the sense of God’s mercy. They mark that the doxology here springs directly from the redemption just named: having spoken of Christ giving himself and the Father’s gracious will, Paul cannot but ascribe to God the glory for so great a salvation. They observe Calvin’s point that this sudden thanksgiving is designed to awaken in the Galatians a fresh sense of the invaluable gift they had received — and so to prepare their minds, by gratitude, for the rebuke that is coming. They note the fitness of the praise: a deliverance wrought wholly by grace leaves all the glory to God, none to man or to the works of the law. The verse thus closes the opening confession of the gospel with worship, and turns the Galatians’ thoughts to the greatness of the grace they were in danger of abandoning. The doxology, by exalting the freeness of redemption, sharpens the wonder and grief of the abrupt reproach that immediately follows.

So the witnesses see Paul’s heart overflow into doxology at the thought of grace. They note his habit of bursting into praise mid-argument, so full is he of God’s mercy; the doxology springs from the redemption just named. Calvin’s point is that this sudden thanksgiving awakens in the Galatians a fresh sense of the invaluable gift received, preparing their minds for the rebuke to come. A deliverance wrought wholly by grace leaves all the glory to God, none to man or the works of the law — sharpening the wonder of the abrupt reproach that follows.
6:
On Galatians 1:6 — “I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel” — the witnesses mark the abrupt reproach.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the tone. Robertson: “ye are so quickly removing” (present middle, transferring yourselves) — from the time the Judaizers came. Ellicott: the Greek word is used of a “deserter” or “turn-coat”; the tense is strictly present, “in the act of falling away.” The Puritan Henry notes how deeply he was concerned. The Lutheran Luther: Paul handles them “after a very fatherly sort.”

The verse expresses Paul’s astonishment. He marvels that the Galatians are so quickly deserting the God who called them by grace, turning to a different gospel. Their defection is swift and grievous.

So the witnesses see Paul, instead of his usual thanksgiving for his readers, break out in astonished reproof. The witnesses note the omission of the customary praise — the gravity of the case allows none — and the word of wonder, “I marvel.” They mark, with Luther, that even here Paul begins gently, in a fatherly way, marvelling rather than thundering, though the offence is grave. They observe the force of the tense the expositors press: not merely that the Galatians had fallen, but that they were “so soon” and even now in the very act of falling away — deserting, like turncoats, the God who had called them. They note the sharpness of what they were leaving and turning to: from “him that called you into the grace of Christ” — the gracious God and his free gospel — “unto another gospel,” the legalism of the Judaizers. The verse thus opens the body of the letter with grieved amazement: that a people so freely called by grace should so quickly forsake it for a gospel of works. The wonder and sorrow set the stage for Paul’s insistence that this “other gospel” is no gospel at all.

So the witnesses see Paul, instead of his usual thanksgiving, break out in astonished reproof. They note the word of wonder, “I marvel,” and, with Luther, that even here he begins gently, in a fatherly way. They mark the tense the expositors press: not merely that the Galatians had fallen, but that they were “so soon” and even now in the very act of falling away — deserting, like turncoats, the God who called them “into the grace of Christ” for “another gospel,” the Judaizers’ legalism. The wonder and sorrow set the stage.
7:
On Galatians 1:7 — “Which is not another; but there be some that trouble you, and would pervert the gospel of Christ” — the witnesses mark the verdict on the false gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the correction. Robertson: “which is not another” (allo) — it is no “gospel” at all, but “a yoke of bondage… the abolition of grace”; there is but one gospel, of grace not works. Adam Clarke: it “differs most essentially” — it loads you again with burdens. Wesley: “no gospel at all… not glad, but heavy, tidings.”

The verse corrects the term “another gospel.” It is not really another, for there is only one; but certain men trouble the Galatians and would pervert the true gospel of Christ. The legalism is a corruption, not a rival gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul deny that the false teaching deserves the name of gospel at all. The witnesses note the careful correction: he had said “another gospel,” but now adds “which is not another” — for there can be but one true gospel, and what the Judaizers offer is no good news but a yoke of bondage, the very abolition of grace. They mark Wesley’s point that it is not glad tidings but heavy, setting acceptance with God on terms impossible to fulfil. They observe that Paul identifies the culprits: “some that trouble you” — disturbers who unsettle the churches — “and would pervert the gospel of Christ,” twisting and corrupting it by adding the works of the law to faith. They note that to mix law and grace in justification is, in effect, to destroy the gospel, for it overturns its very principle. The verse thus sharpens the rebuke of verse 6: the Galatians are not choosing between two gospels but abandoning the one true gospel for a corruption of it. There is no second gospel; there is only the gospel of grace and its perversion — and against that perversion Paul will pronounce his anathema.

So the witnesses see Paul deny that the false teaching deserves the name of gospel at all. He had said “another gospel,” but adds “which is not another” — for there can be but one true gospel, and what the Judaizers offer is no good news but a yoke of bondage, the abolition of grace. Wesley notes it is heavy, not glad, tidings. Paul names the culprits: “some that trouble you” would “pervert the gospel of Christ” by adding works to faith. To mix law and grace in justification destroys the gospel — and against that perversion Paul pronounces his anathema.
8:
On Galatians 1:8 — “But though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you than that which we have preached unto you, let him be accursed” — the witnesses mark the great anathema.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sweep. Robertson: “suppose I (literary plural) should turn renegade and preach… contrary to that which we preached.” Ellicott: “even though” marks an extreme, improbable supposition. Adam Clarke: even if the false teachers claim an angel’s authority, “let him be accursed.” Wesley: “accursed — cut off from Christ and God.”

The verse pronounces a curse on any preacher of a false gospel. Even if Paul himself, or an angel from heaven, should preach another gospel, let him be accursed. The truth stands above every authority.

So the witnesses see Paul subordinate every authority, even his own and an angel’s, to the one true gospel. The witnesses note the staggering extremity of the supposition: not merely that false teachers, but that Paul himself, or even an angel from heaven, should preach a different gospel — an impossible case, put to show that the gospel does not derive its truth from the messenger, however exalted, but stands above all. They mark the verdict: “let him be accursed” — anathema, devoted to destruction, cut off from Christ. They observe the force against the Judaizers, some of whom may have claimed angelic or apostolic sanction: no credential, however high, can validate a corrupted gospel; the message judges the messenger, not the reverse. They note that this fixes the gospel as the unalterable standard, against which even apostles and angels are measured. The verse thus places the truth of the gospel above all persons and powers: to tamper with it is to incur God’s curse, be the offender who he may. It is the strongest possible assertion of the gospel’s finality — repeated, for emphasis, in the verse that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul subordinate every authority, even his own and an angel’s, to the one true gospel. The staggering supposition — that Paul himself, or an angel from heaven, should preach a different gospel — shows the gospel does not derive its truth from the messenger, however exalted, but stands above all. “Let him be accursed” — anathema, cut off from Christ. No credential, however high, can validate a corrupted gospel; the message judges the messenger. The gospel is the unalterable standard, against which even apostles and angels are measured.
9:
On Galatians 1:9 — “As we said before, so say I now again, If any man preach any other gospel unto you than that ye have received, let him be accursed” — the witnesses mark the repeated curse.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the deliberate repetition. Robertson: Paul “will stand by it to the end” — not mere excitement but a deliberate statement. The Reformed Calvin: he repeats that it is unlawful to teach anything contrary to what they learned. The Lutheran Luther: he “repeateth the self-same thing,” now of “any” who preach another gospel. Adam Clarke: such a person should “have no countenance” in his work.

The verse repeats the anathema for emphasis. As Paul has said, so he says again: anyone who preaches a gospel other than the one received is accursed. The solemn curse is doubled.

So the witnesses see Paul deliberately repeat the curse, that none may think it spoken in passion. The witnesses note that he reaffirms what he has just said, “as we said before, so say I now again” — a calculated, not impulsive, statement, by which he commits himself to it without retreat. They mark the slight shift the expositors observe: in verse 8 he named himself and angels; here he speaks of “any man” — bringing the curse home directly to the Judaizers and any who, like them, preach a gospel other than the one the Galatians received. They observe the standard: the gospel “ye have received” — the apostolic message already delivered and believed — is the fixed measure; whatever departs from it is condemned. They note that the repetition underlines the gravity: this is no momentary outburst but Paul’s settled, solemn judgment. The verse thus seals the anathema by doubling it: the one true gospel, received from the apostles, is the unalterable rule, and every preacher of a different gospel falls under God’s curse. With this twice-pronounced sentence Paul vindicates the finality of the gospel before turning to the charge that he sought to please men.

So the witnesses see Paul deliberately repeat the curse, that none may think it spoken in passion. He reaffirms it, “as we said before, so say I now again” — a calculated, not impulsive, statement, by which he commits himself without retreat. They mark the shift: in verse 8 he named himself and angels; here he speaks of “any man,” bringing the curse home to the Judaizers. The standard is the gospel “ye have received” — the apostolic message already delivered and believed. The repetition underlines the gravity: this is Paul’s settled, solemn judgment.
10:
On Galatians 1:10 — “For do I now persuade men, or God? or do I seek to please men? for if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ” — the witnesses mark Paul’s answer to the charge of men-pleasing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “am I persuading?” (conative), trying to win favour. Adam Clarke: peithein ton Theon, to court or solicit favour — as a persecutor he served men, now Christ. The Puritan Henry: he supports his claim to the apostolic office. Wesley: “is this what I aim at in preaching?” The Lutheran Luther presses the vehemency.

The verse answers a slander. Does Paul seek to win the favour of men, or of God? If he still pleased men, he would not be Christ’s servant. His severe anathema proves he serves Christ, not human approval.

So the witnesses see Paul turn his uncompromising curse into proof that he is no men-pleaser. The witnesses note the implied charge: his opponents had perhaps accused him of trimming his message to win favour, of softening the law to please the Gentiles. The very vehemence of his anathema refutes this: a man so willing to pronounce a curse, even on apostles and angels, plainly does not court human approval. They mark the contrast he draws with his former life: before his conversion he was a man-pleaser, serving the opinions of men; “now” he seeks only God’s favour. They observe the decisive principle: “if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ” — the two are incompatible; to make pleasing men the aim is to forfeit the service of Christ. They note that faithful ministry must often displease men for God’s sake. The verse thus vindicates Paul’s integrity: his boldness is the badge of a true servant of Christ, not a flatterer of men — and prepares his fuller defence of the divine, independent origin of his gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul turn his uncompromising curse into proof that he is no men-pleaser. The implied charge was that he trimmed his message to win favour; the very vehemence of his anathema refutes it. They mark the contrast with his former life: before conversion he was a man-pleaser; “now” he seeks only God’s favour. The decisive principle: “if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ” — the two are incompatible. His boldness is the badge of a true servant of Christ, not a flatterer — preparing his defence of the gospel’s divine origin.
11:
On Galatians 1:11 — “But I certify you, brethren, that the gospel which was preached of me is not after man” — the witnesses mark the divine, not human, origin of his gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the thesis. Robertson: “the gospel which was gospelized by me… is not after a human standard.” Adam Clarke: “not a spark of human invention… nor… human cunning.” Wesley: “not suited to the taste of man.” The Lutheran Luther: here is “the principal point… a confutation of his adversaries,” running to the end of chapter two.

The verse states the proposition Paul will prove. The gospel he preached is not of human origin or fashioning. It came not from man but from God.

So the witnesses see Paul state the great thesis of his self-defence: his gospel is not after man. The witnesses note the affectionate “brethren,” restored now as he turns to instruct rather than rebuke, and the solemn “I certify” — I make known, I assure you. They mark the claim: the gospel he preached is “not after man” — neither of human invention nor measured by human standards nor suited to human taste; it bears no mark of man’s cunning or wisdom. They observe Luther’s point that this is the principal hinge of the whole argument, defended from here to the end of chapter two: if Paul’s gospel is divine in origin, then it cannot be corrected by the Judaizers or any man, and to forsake it is to forsake God. They note that the independence of his message from human sources is the ground of its authority. The verse thus announces the theme of the autobiographical defence that follows: Paul did not receive his gospel from men, but directly from God — a claim he will prove by the history of his conversion and his dealings with the apostles. The divine origin of the gospel is the foundation of its finality.

So the witnesses see Paul state the great thesis of his self-defence: his gospel is not after man. They note the affectionate “brethren,” restored now as he turns to instruct, and the solemn “I certify.” The gospel he preached is “not after man” — neither of human invention nor measured by human standards nor suited to human taste. Luther calls this the principal hinge of the whole argument, defended to the end of chapter two: if Paul’s gospel is divine in origin, it cannot be corrected by the Judaizers or any man, and to forsake it is to forsake God.
12:
On Galatians 1:12 — “For I neither received it of man, neither was I taught it, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the source of his gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the double denial. Robertson: “nor was I taught it” — not from Peter or any apostle, nor in Gamaliel’s school; he “received” it by direct revelation. Adam Clarke: “commissioned by himself alone… from Christ crucified.” Wesley: not received “at once” nor “taught it… gradually, by any man,” but by Christ’s revelation.

The verse states how Paul came by his gospel. He did not receive it from any man, nor was he taught it, but received it by the revelation of Jesus Christ. Its source is wholly divine.

So the witnesses see Paul deny both possible human channels and affirm the divine one. The witnesses note the careful double denial: he did not “receive” the gospel from man — not handed to him as a tradition by Peter or another — “neither was he taught it” — not instructed in it gradually as a pupil, whether by the apostles or in the rabbinic school of Gamaliel. They mark the positive source: “by the revelation of Jesus Christ” — the risen Lord himself unveiled the gospel to him, on the Damascus road and in the period that followed. They observe that this shuts out the Judaizers’ insinuation that Paul’s gospel was a second-hand, defective version derived from the Jerusalem apostles, to be corrected by them. They note that the immediacy of his revelation places his gospel on the same footing as that of the twelve, who also received from Christ. The verse thus grounds the thesis of verse 11: Paul’s gospel is not after man because it came not through man but straight from Christ. The divine revelation is the warrant of his apostleship and the unalterable authority of his message — which he will now confirm by the narrative of his life.

So the witnesses see Paul deny both human channels and affirm the divine one. He did not “receive” the gospel from man — not handed to him as tradition by Peter or another — “neither was he taught it” gradually as a pupil, whether by the apostles or in Gamaliel’s school. The positive source: “by the revelation of Jesus Christ” — the risen Lord himself unveiled the gospel to him. This shuts out the Judaizers’ insinuation that his gospel was a second-hand, defective version from Jerusalem, to be corrected by them. The divine revelation is the warrant of his apostleship.
13:
On Galatians 1:13 — “For ye have heard of my conversation in time past in the Jews’ religion, how that beyond measure I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it” — the witnesses mark Paul’s former life as proof.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the appeal to fact. Robertson: “my manner of life” (anastrophe) “in the Jews’ religion.” Adam Clarke points to Acts 9:1; Acts 22:4. The Reformed Calvin: his abhorrence of the gospel shows “his conversion was divine.” Ellicott notes Paul usually brought his own career into his preaching (1 Corinthians 15:8).

The verse recalls Paul’s past as a persecutor. They had heard how, in his former life in Judaism, he violently persecuted and ravaged the church of God. His history disproves any human source for his gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul begin to prove the divine origin of his gospel from the facts of his life, starting with his former enmity. The witnesses note that his past was notorious and known to the Galatians, who had heard — perhaps from Paul himself, who often recounted his story — of his “conversation,” his manner of life, in zealous Judaism. They mark the violence of it: “beyond measure I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it” — he ravaged it like a destroyer, hauling believers to prison and death. They observe the force of the argument: a man so fiercely opposed to the Christian faith could not have come by the gospel through any natural process of instruction or sympathy; only a divine intervention could have turned such an enemy into a preacher. They note Calvin’s point that the very abhorrence Paul once had proves his conversion was God’s work. The verse thus opens the autobiographical proof: Paul’s gospel is not from man, for in his former life he was the church’s mortal foe — the last man to receive or invent the faith by human means. His persecuting past is the dark backdrop against which the divine grace of his calling will shine.

So the witnesses see Paul begin to prove the divine origin of his gospel from the facts of his life, starting with his former enmity. His past was notorious: the Galatians had heard of his “conversation” in zealous Judaism, and the violence of it — “beyond measure I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it.” The force of the argument: a man so fiercely opposed to the faith could not have come by the gospel through any natural process; only a divine intervention could have turned such an enemy into a preacher. The persecuting past is the dark backdrop against which grace will shine.
14:
On Galatians 1:14 — “And profited in the Jews’ religion above many my equals in mine own nation, being more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers” — the witnesses mark his zeal and advancement.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the progress. Robertson: “I advanced” (proekopton, to blaze a way ahead) — a brilliant pupil under Gamaliel (Philippians 3:4–6), in the lead of the persecution. Adam Clarke: more skilled “in the traditions of the fathers” than his fellow students. Wesley: “zealous of the unwritten traditions.” The Lutheran Luther notes he calls even Moses’ law the “fathers’ traditions.”

The verse adds his eminence in Judaism. Paul advanced beyond his peers, surpassingly zealous for the ancestral traditions. He was a leader, not a laggard, in the religion he afterward forsook.

So the witnesses see Paul press the proof further: he was no half-hearted Jew won over by weakness, but a foremost zealot. The witnesses note his advancement — he “profited” or progressed in Judaism “above many” of his own age and nation, a brilliant pupil who outstripped his fellows. They mark the engine of it: “more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers” — an intense, surpassing devotion to the ancestral law and the traditions of the elders, which drove his persecution of the church. They observe the weight this adds to the argument: a man so deeply committed to Judaism, so advanced and so zealous, had every human reason to remain its champion, not to abandon it for the faith he hated. Only an overpowering act of God could have wrested such a man from such a course. They note Luther’s observation that Paul honours even the law as the fathers’ tradition, yet forsook reliance on it for Christ. The verse thus completes the picture of his former life: the more eminent his zeal for the law, the more clearly his conversion to the gospel of grace must be God’s doing, not man’s — setting up the great “but when it pleased God” that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul press the proof further: he was no half-hearted Jew won over by weakness, but a foremost zealot. He “profited” in Judaism above many of his own age, a brilliant pupil who outstripped his fellows, “more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers.” The weight this adds: a man so deeply committed had every human reason to remain its champion, not to abandon it for the faith he hated. Only an overpowering act of God could have wrested such a man from such a course — setting up the great “but when it pleased God” that follows.
15:
On Galatians 1:15 — “But when it pleased God, who separated me from my mother’s womb, and called me by his grace” — the witnesses mark the sovereign grace of his calling.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the divine initiative. Robertson: “it was the good pleasure of God” (eudokesen); “who separated me” (aphorisas), marked off from birth. Adam Clarke: God who “preserved me… an infant… and saved me by his grace… an adult persecutor.” Wesley: set apart for an apostle, as Jeremiah for a prophet (Jeremiah 1:5). The Puritan Henry: all savingly converted are “called by the grace of God.”

The verse traces all to God’s good pleasure. When it pleased God, who had set Paul apart from birth and called him by grace, the great change began. His conversion sprang from sovereign grace.

So the witnesses see Paul ascribe his conversion wholly to the free pleasure and grace of God. The witnesses note the turning-point — “but when it pleased God” — the great contrast to his former zeal: the change came not from himself but from God’s good pleasure, his sovereign purpose. They mark the depth of that purpose: God “separated me from my mother’s womb” — set him apart, like Jeremiah, before birth, destining him for the apostleship even while he was yet a persecutor. They observe the means: “called me by his grace” — not for any merit, endeavour, or worthiness in Paul, but by free, unmerited favour; the chief of persecutors was called by grace alone. They note Henry’s point that all who are savingly converted are so called, by grace working in them. The verse thus locates the source of Paul’s gospel and apostleship in God’s eternal, gracious choice: his calling was no human arrangement but the unfolding of a divine purpose set before his birth and accomplished by grace. This sovereign call, against all his natural inclination, proves once more that his gospel is from God — and leads to its purpose, the revealing of Christ in him.

So the witnesses see Paul ascribe his conversion wholly to the free pleasure and grace of God. The turning-point, “but when it pleased God,” is the great contrast to his former zeal: the change came not from himself but from God’s sovereign purpose. That purpose ran deep: God “separated me from my mother’s womb” — set him apart, like Jeremiah, before birth. The means: “called me by his grace” — not for any merit, but by free, unmerited favour; the chief of persecutors called by grace alone. His calling was no human arrangement but the unfolding of a divine purpose.
16:
On Galatians 1:16 — “To reveal his Son in me, that I might preach him among the heathen; immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood” — the witnesses mark the purpose and the independence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Robertson: “in me” (en emoi) may stress his inward experience of grace or “in my case.” Ellicott: “in my mind, or consciousness.” Adam Clarke: “to make me know Jesus Christ… that I might preach him among the heathen.” Wesley: he conferred not with flesh and blood, “being fully satisfied.”

The verse gives the aim of his calling and his immediate response. God revealed his Son in Paul, that he might preach him among the Gentiles; and at once Paul consulted no human being. The revelation was direct, the obedience prompt and independent.

So the witnesses see the purpose of Paul’s calling and his immediate, unconsulting obedience. The witnesses note the end of God’s gracious act: “to reveal his Son in me” — to unveil Christ both to Paul (in the Damascus vision) and within him (in the inward illumination of grace), so that he might know the Son truly; and “that I might preach him among the heathen” — the very commission to the Gentiles which the Judaizers disputed. They mark the decisive proof of independence: “immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood” — Paul did not, upon his calling, run to any man for instruction or authorization; he did not seek the gospel from human teachers. They observe that this seals the argument of verse 12: having received Christ by revelation, he needed no human confirmation and sought none. They note that the inward revelation of the Son qualified him to preach — one must know Christ to make him known. The verse thus joins the divine purpose of his calling to its independent execution: God revealed his Son in Paul for the Gentile mission, and Paul, certain of his commission, acted at once without consulting men — the first of the proofs that his gospel owes nothing to human authority.

So the witnesses see the purpose of Paul’s calling and his immediate, unconsulting obedience. The end of God’s gracious act: “to reveal his Son in me” — to unveil Christ both to Paul and within him — “that I might preach him among the heathen,” the very Gentile commission the Judaizers disputed. The proof of independence: “immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood” — he did not run to any man for instruction or authorization. Having received Christ by revelation, he needed no human confirmation and sought none — the first of the proofs that his gospel owes nothing to human authority.
17:
On Galatians 1:17 — “Neither went I up to Jerusalem to them which were apostles before me; but I went into Arabia, and returned again unto Damascus” — the witnesses mark his avoidance of the apostles.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the movements. Robertson: the Jerusalem apostles were genuine, “but so is Paul”; his call came not from them; the Arabian visit falls between two stays in Damascus. Adam Clarke: he was “appointed by the same authority… as the rest of the apostles.” The Reformed Calvin: he “did not ask the authority of any man,” not even the apostles.

The verse stresses that Paul did not seek the apostles’ sanction. He did not go up to Jerusalem to those who were apostles before him, but withdrew to Arabia and returned to Damascus. He sought no human ratification of his call.

So the witnesses see Paul press the proof of independence: he did not, after his calling, hasten to the apostles for approval. The witnesses note the pointed denial — he did not “go up to Jerusalem to them which were apostles before me” — acknowledging that the twelve were apostles before him, yet insisting that he did not derive his commission or his gospel from them. They mark his actual course: “I went into Arabia, and returned again unto Damascus” — a withdrawal, perhaps for meditation and the deepening of his revelation, far from the Jerusalem leaders. They observe the force against the Judaizers: had Paul received his gospel from the twelve, he would have gone straight to them; instead he kept apart, proving that his message came from Christ, not from human teachers. They note Calvin’s point that he sought no man’s authority, not even the apostles’. The verse thus advances the autobiographical argument: in the crucial early days, Paul neither consulted nor was instructed by the apostles, but acted on his direct commission from Christ. His independence of Jerusalem is the proof that his gospel is divine, not derived — a point he confirms by detailing the long delay before he ever met Peter.

So the witnesses see Paul press the proof of independence: he did not hasten to the apostles for approval. He did not “go up to Jerusalem to them which were apostles before me” — acknowledging the twelve, yet insisting he did not derive his commission from them. Instead, “I went into Arabia, and returned again unto Damascus” — a withdrawal far from the Jerusalem leaders. Had Paul received his gospel from the twelve, he would have gone straight to them; instead he kept apart, proving his message came from Christ. He sought no man’s authority, not even the apostles’ (Calvin).
18:
On Galatians 1:18 — “Then after three years I went up to Jerusalem to see Peter, and abode with him fifteen days” — the witnesses mark the first, brief visit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the time and purpose. Robertson: “after three years” (a round number from his departure for Damascus); “to visit Cephas” (historesai, to get acquainted). Adam Clarke: “to become personally acquainted with Peter.” The Lutheran Luther: he went “not to learn any thing of them, but to see Peter.” The Reformed Calvin: not till three years after he began the apostolic office.

The verse records the first Jerusalem visit, long delayed and brief. Only after three years did Paul go up to see Peter, staying just fifteen days. The lateness and shortness prove his independence.

So the witnesses see Paul date and limit his first contact with the apostles, to show how little he owed them. The witnesses note the long delay: “after three years” — reckoned from his conversion or departure for Damascus — he had preached and ministered for years before ever seeing a Jerusalem apostle, so his gospel could not have come from them. They mark the purpose: “to see Peter” — the word means to visit, to become acquainted, not to be instructed; he went as an equal seeking fellowship, not as a pupil seeking doctrine. They observe the brevity: “fifteen days” — too short a time to be schooled in the gospel, even had that been his aim; a fortnight’s acquaintance, not a course of training. They note Luther’s point that he went to see Peter, not to learn from him. The verse thus furnishes another proof of independence: Paul’s first meeting with an apostle came late and lasted briefly, a visit of fellowship, not of instruction. His gospel was already formed and preached before he ever spoke with Peter — confirming that it came from Christ, not from the Jerusalem church.

So the witnesses see Paul date and limit his first contact with the apostles, to show how little he owed them. The long delay — “after three years” — means he had preached for years before ever seeing a Jerusalem apostle. The purpose: “to see Peter” — to visit, to become acquainted, not to be instructed; he went as an equal seeking fellowship, not as a pupil seeking doctrine. The brevity, “fifteen days,” was too short to be schooled in the gospel. His message was already formed and preached before he ever spoke with Peter.
19:
On Galatians 1:19 — “But other of the apostles saw I none, save James the Lord’s brother” — the witnesses mark the one other apostle seen.

The grammatical-historical commentators note James. Robertson: “James the son of Zebedee was still living… James, brother of the Lord, is here termed an apostle.” Barnes and Clarke discuss the two Jameses. Ellicott: in what sense an apostle depends on who he was. The Reformed Calvin: almost all the ancients agree he was “the Just,” who presided over the Jerusalem church.

The verse limits Paul’s Jerusalem contacts. Besides Peter, he saw no other apostle except James the Lord’s brother. His acquaintance with the apostles was minimal.

So the witnesses see Paul stress how few of the apostles he even met. The witnesses note the limitation: on that first visit he saw only Peter and, besides him, none of the apostles “save James the Lord’s brother” — the James who led the Jerusalem church, reckoned among the apostles in the broad sense. They mark the question the expositors raise over this James — whether the Lord’s brother is identical with James the son of Alphaeus, or a distinct “brother” (cousin or kinsman) of Jesus — the ancients commonly calling him “the Just,” head of the mother church. They observe the force of the detail: Paul names exactly whom he saw, to show that his exposure to the apostolic circle was slight — two men, briefly — far too little to be the source of his gospel. They note the precision serves his argument and his honesty alike. The verse thus tightens the proof: Paul met but two of the leading men, and that briefly, so that his gospel cannot be traced to the Jerusalem apostles as a body. His message remained his own, received from Christ — a point so important that he will now confirm it with a solemn oath.

So the witnesses see Paul stress how few of the apostles he even met. On that first visit he saw only Peter and, besides him, none of the apostles “save James the Lord’s brother” — the James who led the Jerusalem church, reckoned an apostle in the broad sense. They mark the question over this James — whether the Lord’s brother is the son of Alphaeus or a distinct kinsman of Jesus, commonly called “the Just.” Paul names exactly whom he saw, to show his exposure to the apostolic circle was slight — two men, briefly — far too little to be the source of his gospel.
20:
On Galatians 1:20 — “Now the things which I write unto you, behold, before God, I lie not” — the witnesses mark the solemn oath.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the asseveration. Robertson: “so important does he deem the point that he takes solemn oath about it.” Barnes: an oath, “to prevent all suspicion of falsehood” (Romans 9:1). The Reformed Calvin: an oath “cannot lawfully be employed but on great and weighty occasions.” The Lutheran Luther: he swears that the churches might believe him and the false apostles not gainsay him.

The verse confirms the narrative with an oath. Paul calls God to witness that he does not lie. The matter is weighty enough to swear to.

So the witnesses see Paul seal his account with a solemn appeal to God. The witnesses note the gravity this implies: Paul interrupts his narrative to swear, “before God, I lie not” — calling the Searcher of hearts to witness the truth of what he has written about his independence from the apostles. They mark that, as Calvin observes, an oath is lawful only on great and weighty occasions, and Paul’s use of one here shows how crucial this point is to the whole dispute: if his gospel came directly from Christ and not from men, the Judaizers’ case collapses. They observe Luther’s reason for the oath: to secure the churches’ belief and to stop the mouths of the false apostles, who might otherwise question whether Paul spoke the truth. They note that the appeal to God underlines Paul’s transparent sincerity, leaving no room for doubt. The verse thus places the seal of an oath upon the autobiographical proof: the facts of his early independence are sworn before God, beyond cavil. So weighty is the divine origin of his gospel that Paul stakes his veracity, in God’s presence, upon the record — before continuing the account of his obscurity to the Judean churches.

So the witnesses see Paul seal his account with a solemn appeal to God. He interrupts the narrative to swear, “before God, I lie not” — calling the Searcher of hearts to witness the truth of what he has written about his independence from the apostles. As Calvin observes, an oath is lawful only on weighty occasions, and Paul’s use of one shows how crucial this point is: if his gospel came directly from Christ, the Judaizers’ case collapses. Luther adds that he swears to secure the churches’ belief and stop the false apostles’ mouths. So weighty is the divine origin of his gospel.
21:
On Galatians 1:21 — “Afterwards I came into the regions of Syria and Cilicia” — the witnesses mark his departure from Judea.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the movement. Robertson: “the region of Syria and Cilicia” (cf. Acts 9:30); Paul was “at work in Tarsus and the surrounding country.” Adam Clarke traces the course: Damascus to Jerusalem, then Syria and Cilicia, after the Hellenists sought to kill him (Acts 9:29). Barnes notes Luke records the controversy he omits here.

The verse follows Paul out of Judea. After the brief Jerusalem visit, he went into the regions of Syria and Cilicia. He soon left the apostles’ territory for distant fields.

So the witnesses see Paul trace his withdrawal from Judea into far regions, away from the apostles. The witnesses note the geography: after the fortnight with Peter, Paul departed for “the regions of Syria and Cilicia” — to Antioch and his native Tarsus, far from Jerusalem and the twelve. They mark the background Luke supplies: at Jerusalem the Hellenistic Jews had sought to kill him, and the brethren sent him away, so that he laboured in distant fields rather than under the apostles’ eye. They observe the bearing on the argument: Paul spent these formative years working at a distance from the Jerusalem leaders, not in their company or tutelage, which again shows that his gospel and ministry did not depend on them. They note that he was not idle but at work, preaching in Syria and Cilicia. The verse thus continues the proof of independence by geography: Paul ministered far from Judea, removed from the apostles, so that neither his message nor his commission could be ascribed to them. His obscurity to the Judean churches, which the next verses describe, follows naturally from this distance — and itself testifies to the divine, not human, source of his gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul trace his withdrawal from Judea into far regions, away from the apostles. After the fortnight with Peter, he departed for “the regions of Syria and Cilicia” — to Antioch and his native Tarsus. The background Luke supplies: at Jerusalem the Hellenistic Jews had sought to kill him, and the brethren sent him away, so he laboured in distant fields rather than under the apostles’ eye. He spent these formative years working at a distance, not in their company or tutelage — again showing his gospel and ministry did not depend on them.
22:
On Galatians 1:22 — “And was unknown by face unto the churches of Judaea which were in Christ” — the witnesses mark his obscurity in Judea.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the point. Robertson: “I was still unknown” (periphrastic imperfect) “by face” to the Judean churches, distinct from Jerusalem. Barnes: he had formed no acquaintance with the churches there. Adam Clarke: “I was not personally acquainted with any of the Churches of Judea.” The Reformed Calvin sees this as heightening the wickedness of his slanderers.

The verse notes that the Judean churches did not even know Paul by sight. He remained personally unknown to the congregations of Judea. He could not have learned his gospel from them.

So the witnesses see Paul add that not even the Judean churches knew him personally. The witnesses note the further proof of independence: beyond the apostles, Paul might have been thought to have derived his gospel from the Christian congregations of Judea; but he was “unknown by face” to them — they had never so much as seen him, let alone instructed him. They mark the distinction from Jerusalem: he had briefly visited that city, but the wider churches of Judea remained strangers to him in person. They observe that this closes off the last human channel: neither from the apostles at Jerusalem nor from the believers of Judea could Paul have received his message, for with the one his contact was brief and with the other non-existent. They note Calvin’s point that this very obscurity heightens the malice of those who slandered him, for the Judean churches themselves, far from teaching him, marvelled at his conversion. The verse thus completes the proof from his early life: Paul was personally unknown in Judea, so his gospel cannot be traced to any human source there — setting up the report that reached those churches, and their wonder at the grace shown in him.

So the witnesses see Paul add that not even the Judean churches knew him personally. He might have been thought to have derived his gospel from the Christian congregations of Judea; but he was “unknown by face” to them — they had never so much as seen him, let alone instructed him. He had briefly visited Jerusalem, but the wider churches remained strangers to him in person. This closes off the last human channel: neither from the apostles nor from the believers of Judea could Paul have received his message. The obscurity heightens the malice of those who slandered him (Calvin).
23:
On Galatians 1:23 — “But they had heard only, That he which persecuted us in times past now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed” — the witnesses mark the report of his conversion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rumour. Robertson: “they were only hearing from time to time” (periphrastic imperfect); “the one who used to persecute us” (Acts 9:13; 1 Timothy 1:13). Ellicott: “the faith” here is the one cardinal doctrine of faith in Christ. Adam Clarke: as a persecutor he was well known, and as a convert no less so.

The verse gives what the Judean churches knew of Paul: only by report. They had merely heard that their former persecutor now preached the faith he once destroyed. His fame had reached them, though his face had not.

So the witnesses see the Judean churches knowing Paul only by the astonishing report of his change. The witnesses note the contrast with personal acquaintance: they did not know him by sight, but they “had heard” — the news kept reaching them — of the remarkable thing God had done. They mark the substance of the report, framed in the churches’ own wondering words: “he which persecuted us in times past now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed” — the very man who had ravaged the church was now proclaiming the gospel he had tried to wipe out. They observe the force of this for Paul’s argument: the change was so complete and so manifestly divine that it became famous, the talk of the churches, proof that no human teaching but only God’s power could account for it. They note that “the faith” he now preached was the very faith he once destroyed — the same gospel, now from the lips of its former foe. The verse thus turns Paul’s obscurity to account: known only by report, he was known precisely as the persecutor-turned-preacher, a living testimony to the grace that called him — a wonder that moved the churches to praise.

So the witnesses see the Judean churches knowing Paul only by the astonishing report of his change. They did not know him by sight, but “had heard” — the news kept reaching them — of the remarkable thing God had done. The substance, framed in the churches’ own wondering words: “he which persecuted us in times past now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed.” The change was so complete and so manifestly divine that it became famous, the talk of the churches, proof that no human teaching but only God’s power could account for it — a living testimony to the grace that called him.
24:
On Galatians 1:24 — “And they glorified God in me” — the witnesses mark the churches’ praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the response. Robertson: “they glorified” (imperfect, kept on doing it) “in me… in my case.” Barnes: they praised God on his account, regarding him as a true convert. Ellicott: the proper attitude of Christian admiration — it “does not rest in him… but… giving praise to God.” Adam Clarke: “I owe nothing to them; I owe all to God; and they themselves acknowledge this.”

The verse closes the chapter with the churches’ worship. They glorified God on Paul’s account. His conversion turned their wonder into praise of God.

So the witnesses see the Judean churches respond to Paul’s story not with envy but with praise to God. The witnesses note the lovely conclusion: hearing that their former persecutor now preached the faith, they “glorified God in me” — they kept on praising God for the grace shown in Paul’s conversion and ministry. They mark Clarke’s point that this very response confirms Paul’s argument: the churches ascribed his change to God, not to any human teacher; they owned that he owed all to God, and so, by implication, that his gospel was divine. They observe Ellicott’s note on the right attitude of Christian admiration — the praise does not rest in the man but rises to God who wrought in him. They note the contrast with the Judaizers, who disparaged Paul, while the Judean believers glorified God for him. The verse thus ends the first chapter on a note of worship that crowns the proof of independence: the churches of Judea, far from being Paul’s teachers, were his admirers, praising God for the grace that made the persecutor a preacher. His gospel and apostleship are vindicated as God’s own work — and the narrative will continue, in chapter two, with his later dealings with the apostles.

So the witnesses see the Judean churches respond not with envy but with praise to God. Hearing that their former persecutor now preached the faith, they “glorified God in me” — they kept on praising God for the grace shown in his conversion. Clarke notes this very response confirms the argument: the churches ascribed his change to God, not a human teacher, owning he owed all to God. Ellicott marks the right attitude of admiration — the praise rests not in the man but rises to God. Far from being Paul’s teachers, the Judean churches were his admirers, vindicating his gospel as God’s own work.
